macro_segments.title: Introduction and Return to Kesamim: The Principle of Tliyah (Attribution)

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and transition to Kesamim discussion
Introduction to Tliyah: Attribution to parties who don't lose (butchers, husbands with bloody noses, women with wounds)
Mishnah on lending garments to non-Jewish women and Niddot: Hishilah Chaluka and the principle of convenience
Discussion of whether anyone loses by attribution; distinction between Niddah and non-Jewish woman

macro_segments.title: Three Women Sharing a Garment: When Equal Parties Cannot Attribute

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Gimel Nashim Shelovshu Chaluk Echad (three women sharing one garment or sitting on one chair)
The principle that when attribution is impossible among equals, all become Temei'ot
Comparison to the two roads case: when probability assumes Tahor but multiple candidates eliminate leniency
Rabbi Nechemia's position on stone seats and bathing benches: objects that don't receive Tumah

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Nechemia's Principle: Objects That Cannot Receive Tumah Make Women Tahor

micro_segments.title: Kol davar she'eino mekabel tumah eino mekabel ketamim: foundational principle of stain law
Tosafos explanation: stain laws rooted in Tumah v'Taharah of the object, extending to the woman
Contemporary application: plastic, polyester, nylon and mekabel tumah questions in modern halacha
Why Taharot was not fully studied but remains relevant for Hilchot Niddah and other areas

macro_segments.title: Rav's Limitation on Non-Jewish Women: Age and Precedent for Menstruation

micro_segments.title: Amar Rav: non-Jewish women must have previously menstruated, not just be of age
Rav Sheshet's criticism: Rav was dozing, conflicting with the braita
Braita debate: Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbanan on whether non-Jewish woman need prior menstruation or just age appropriateness
Rav Papa clarification: Rabbi Meir is lenient (age suffices), Rabbanan strict (prior menstruation required)

macro_segments.title: Unequal Loss Cases: Shomeret Yom vs. Shomeret Shiva and the Rabbi vs. Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel Debate

micro_segments.title: Braita: Shomeret Yom K'Neged Yom (one day loss) vs. Shomeret Shiva (seven-day restart)
Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel: one person loses less, so attribute to her; she's saved, friend is hurt
Rabbi: even unequal loss means no attribution; both become Temei'ot equally
Cases where both agree: non-loss scenarios (Shomeret Yom Rishon, Dam Tahor post-childbirth, Besulah)

macro_segments.title: Rav Chisda on Two Roads: Tamei and Tahor Going Separate Paths

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda: Tamei v'Tahor walking two paths (one Tamei, one Tahor), debate whether to attribute to Tamei
Rav Adda's challenge: difference from Shomeret Yom case where women are equally Temei'ot vs. one Tamei one Tahor
Rav Chisda vs. Rav Adda interpretation: does Rabbi reject attribution because hurt or because essentially Tahor

macro_segments.title: Doubtful Tamei Person and Doubtful Stains: Extending Attribution Principles

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yosi b'Rabbi Chanina: Tahor v'Talui (doubtful Tamei) going separate paths; attribute to doubtful
Reason: doubtful Tamei must treat himself as Tamei anyway, so no additional loss
Query: Can we attribute a stain (Ketem) to a woman with a stain (Ketam)?

macro_segments.title: Ketem B'Ketem Dispute: Attributing Stains to Women with Existing Stains

micro_segments.title: Query of Rabbi Yochanan: can stains be attributed to women who found stains on other garments
Rabbi's position: no, cannot attribute stain to stain; requires actual Rah (sighting)
Braita contradiction: Ein Tolin Ketem B'Ketem vs. cases allowing attribution
Resolutions: Rabbi vs. Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel; or Rabbi's position on first day vs. second day
Rav Ashi: Lemapre'a vs. Lehaba (retroactive discovery vs. future orientation of status determination)

macro_segments.title: Practical Clarification of Ketem B'Ketem through Textual Reinterpretation

micro_segments.title: Abaye's reframing: Braita permits attribution to non-Jewish woman with stain or woman in Tahor period
Consequence: maintains principle that stains cannot be attributed to women with stains alone

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Nechemia Extended: Mekabel Tumah Requirement in Stain Law

micro_segments.title: Rav Matna: Rabbi Nechemia's reason from verse Veniketa La'aretz Teshev (she sits on ground and is clean)
Rav Huna: Rabbi Nechemia permits even on outer side of pottery vessel (not normally Mekabel Tumah there)
Abaye: Rabbi Nechemia permits on insufficiently sized rags (not Mekabel Tumah due to size)

macro_segments.title: Historical Precedent vs. Rabbi Nechemia: The Case of Two Women Grinding and the Olive Leaf

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's objection: previous case where rabbis declared women Temei'ot contradicts ruling like Rabbi Nechemia
Braita: two women grinding at hand mill; blood under inner or outer woman
Case of blood on olive leaf during oven heating: rabbis declared both women Temei'ot
Resolution: debate of Tanna'im; another braita shows rabbis ruled like Rabbi Nechemia

macro_segments.title: Integration of Kesamim Criteria: Geris, Mekabel Tumah, and Non-Colored Clothes

micro_segments.title: Three major leniencies: minimum size (Geris), Mekabel Tumah requirement, non-colored clothes only
Parallel to Tum'at Nega'im (Tzara'at stains): same three criteria suggest formal legal framework
Question of formal rules vs. practical considerations in Kesamim law

macro_segments.title: Three Women Sleeping in One Bed: Blood Under One, Individual Checking, and Attribution Between Parties

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Shlosh Nashim Yeshenot Be-Mita Achat; blood found under one woman
All three become Temei'ot if they could have moved around during sleep
If one checks and finds herself Niddah, she alone is Tmeia; other two Tahorot
Tolot Zo B'Zo: can attribute stain to one of the others based on likelihood criteria
If none suitable for seeing (all pregnant), they're considered as if suitable; all equally Temei'ot

macro_segments.title: Checking Timing and Niddah Status: Instantaneous vs. Delayed Checks and Attribution

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda: attribution only works if woman checked instantaneously per Shiur Veset
Principle: husband brings Chatat only if wife checked immediately after relations; analogy to Terumah
Bar Pada: timing framework for Chatat vs. Asham Talui vs. exemption based on elapsed time

macro_segments.title: Blood Prevented During Relations: Why Immediate Post-Coital Checking Allows Retroactive Niddah Status

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yoshia's principle: relations prevented blood from emerging; upon separation, blood emerges immediately
Mashal of child and elder on road: restraint by company, then immediate haste when reaching city
Abaye's mashal: finger in eye prevents tears; removal causes immediate tears
Why relations context differs: in other cases (Terumah), delayed check means blood emerged after, not before

macro_segments.title: Tolot Zo B'Zo Cases: Attribution Based on Likelihood Between Unequal Women

micro_segments.title: Case framework: Ubarah v'She'eina Ubarah (pregnant vs. non-pregnant); attribute to non-pregnant
Menika v'She'eina Menika (nursing vs. non-nursing); attribute to non-nursing
Zekena v'She'eina Zekena (postmenopausal vs. premenopausal); attribute to premenopausal
Besulah v'She'eina Besulah (pre-menarche vs. post-menarche); attribute to post-menarche
If all women equal in status, treat as if all could see; all become Temei'ot

topical_tags.term: Shomeret Yom K'Neged Yom (One day loss case)
Rabbi vs. Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel (Attribution debate)
